title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about the relationship between ancient forests and the people who study them. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: Into the Old Growth
content.subtitle: A magazine article about ancient forests and the scientists who study them.
content.text: The first time ecologist Dana Morrow stepped into an old-growth forest, she was a graduate student with a clipboard and a brand-new pair of waterproof boots. She had spent the previous three years studying forest ecosystems from behind a desk, reading about canopy layers and soil composition in journals that smelled faintly of mildew. [31] Nothing in those papers, she would later admit, had prepared her for the overwhelming physical presence of a Douglas fir that had been standing since before the printing press was invented. [/31] || The forest she entered was in the Pacific Northwest of North America, a region where certain trees have been growing, largely undisturbed, for eight hundred years or more. These are not merely old trees. They are engineers of their own environment. A single ancient Douglas fir can support hundreds of species of insects, birds, mosses and fungi — organisms that depend entirely on the specific conditions created by a tree of that age and scale. [32] Remove the tree, and you do not just remove one organism; you collapse an entire web of dependencies that took centuries to assemble. [/32] || Dana's research focuses on what foresters call 'legacy structures': the fallen logs, hollowed trunks, and deep root networks left behind by trees that have died. These structures, she explains, are far from dead themselves. A decomposing log on the forest floor can harbour more biological diversity than many living ecosystems. [33] 'People tend to see a fallen tree as waste,' she told me, 'but in old-growth terms, it's an inheritance.' [/33] || One of the persistent challenges Dana faces is communicating the value of these forests to people who have never seen them. The commercial timber industry has long regarded old-growth trees as the ultimate prize, their vast trunks representing centuries of accumulated wood. Environmental campaigns have sometimes struggled against this framing, partly because abstract arguments about biodiversity are harder to grasp than the visible value of timber. [34] Dana believes the most effective approach is not to argue with economics, but to reframe what an ancient forest is actually worth — functioning as a water filtration system, a carbon store, a buffer against flooding. [/34] || During our walk through the forest, Dana paused frequently — not from tiredness, but from habit. She had developed what she called 'slow looking', a deliberate practice of standing still and observing a single square metre of forest floor for ten minutes at a stretch. [35] To the untrained eye, it appeared to be nothing more than a patch of damp soil and dead leaves. To Dana, it was a record of the past two hundred years, if you knew how to read it. [/35] || Before we left the forest, I asked Dana whether she thought these places would still exist in a hundred years. She was quiet for a moment. [36] 'That depends entirely,' she said finally, 'on whether we decide they matter before it's too late to choose.' [/36]

questions.question: What does the writer suggest about Dana Morrow's academic preparation?
options: A) It had made her overconfident about her ability as a field researcher.
B) It had not truly readied her for the experience of being in an ancient forest.
C) It had focused too much on practical skills rather than theoretical knowledge.
D) It had given her a thorough understanding of what old-growth forests looked like.
questions.explanation: Dana admits that nothing in the academic papers had prepared her for the 'overwhelming physical presence' of the ancient tree, suggesting her preparation was inadequate for the real experience.

questions.question: The writer mentions the removal of an ancient tree in order to
options: A) illustrate how deeply interconnected old-growth ecosystems are.
B) warn readers about the dangers of logging.
C) compare old-growth forests with younger, managed forests.
D) explain why old-growth forests are difficult to replant.
questions.explanation: The writer uses the example of removing a tree to show that it causes the collapse of 'an entire web of dependencies', emphasising how interconnected everything is.

questions.question: When Dana describes a fallen log as 'an inheritance', she means that
options: A) old trees should be preserved so future generations can see them.
B) foresters should receive financial compensation for protecting fallen trees.
C) the biological richness of dead wood is passed on to the living ecosystem.
D) decomposing trees are more commercially valuable than living ones.
questions.explanation: Dana contrasts the public perception of fallen trees as 'waste' with the ecological reality that they pass on biological richness — an 'inheritance' — to the wider ecosystem.

questions.question: What does Dana believe is the best way to protect old-growth forests?
options: A) Presenting scientific data about biodiversity loss to governments.
B) Restricting all commercial activity near protected forest areas.
C) Replacing timber industry arguments with different economic ones.
D) Encouraging more people to visit and experience the forests directly.
questions.explanation: Dana believes the most effective approach is to 'reframe' the forest's worth using different economic arguments (water filtration, carbon storage, flood prevention) rather than fighting economic logic directly.

questions.question: The contrast between 'the untrained eye' and Dana's perspective is used to show
options: A) the difference between scientific and artistic ways of seeing nature.
B) why forest floor ecosystems are particularly difficult to study.
C) that expert knowledge transforms what appears to be ordinary ground.
D) how unskilled observers tend to undervalue natural environments.
questions.explanation: The contrast highlights that what looks like unremarkable 'damp soil and dead leaves' to a layperson becomes a rich historical record to an expert like Dana.

questions.question: Dana's final answer about the future of the forests suggests that she
options: A) thinks governments will eventually act to protect all forests.
B) feels that scientists have already done everything they can.
C) is pessimistic that old-growth forests can be saved.
D) believes protection is possible but requires a timely decision.
questions.explanation: Dana's words 'before it's too late to choose' imply that protection is still possible, but only if people act soon — suggesting cautious hope rather than pessimism or certainty.
